methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $400M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 25.8x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Ursa Major Technologies is a Series E-stage space and aerospace company that has raised $400M+ to date. The valuation universe comprises 6 public space-sector comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers including SpaceX, Sierra Space, and Planet Labs, though direct peer multiples at the Series E stage are limited.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable triangulation approach. Using $400M in total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 1.4x step-up multiplier for Series E progression yields $560M. This method rests on observable venture capital pacing patterns and represents the core quantitative anchor given data constraints. The peer-median approach could not be applied—while 11 same-sector peers exist in our database, none satisfy minimum thresholds for same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation tier (need ≥3 datapoints). Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples (median 25.8x across the 6-ticker public set) is unavailable without the target's revenue, which private companies typically do not disclose.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $476M to $644M, centered on $560M. The wide range reflects reliance on a single method and absence of revenue data to cross-check against public multiples. Key uncertainties include the applicability of generic stage-progression assumptions to frontier aerospace (which may command premium or discount relative to software venture patterns) and the revenue profile needed to benchmark against public comparables.
